acceptance: A heimq release tag (>= v0.1.5) exists that includes the heimq-handlers crate with the four entrypoint groups public and documented as embedder API (codec envelope, produce async-with-context, metadata/api_versions/init_producer_id handlers, RecordBatchView::decode_all); AND SASL ownership is confirmed in writing (embedder-owned vs heimq-owned). Once tagged, niflheim bumps its heimq pin from v0.1.2 to that tag and unblocks niflheim-ba3e609c / niflheim-7271ad92 / niflheim-955504d0.
description:
niflheim is the downstream embedder of the heimq engine. heimq-236c0fc2 (CLOSED, commit 35e8a15) extracted the request-level handlers into the new `heimq-handlers` crate: request-envelope codec (codec::decode_request / decode_request_bytes / encode_response / encode_response_body), plus produce::handle_async_with_context_and_config_store (async pre-ack path), and the metadata / api_versions / init_producer_id handlers — all generic over heimq-broker LogBackend + RequestContext and over the vendored `heimq_protocol` fork. RecordBatchView::decode_all covers legacy v0/v1 message sets + multi-batch. That surface is exactly what niflheim needs to retire its in-tree Kafka codec/handlers.

WHAT NIFLHEIM EXPECTS FROM HEIMQ so it can execute niflheim-ba3e609c (delegate produce decode+dispatch), niflheim-7271ad92 (serve ApiVersions/Metadata/InitProducerId via heimq), and niflheim-955504d0 (delete in-tree codec/handlers):

1. RELEASED TAG THAT BUNDLES heimq-handlers. niflheim pins heimq as released git TAGS (today heimq-broker/heimq-protocol/heimq-wire are all pinned at tag v0.1.2, which PREDATES the extraction). heimq-handlers currently exists only on main. Cut a release tag (>= v0.1.5) that publishes heimq-handlers alongside the other three crates on the SAME tag, so niflheim can add `heimq-handlers = { git = "https://github.com/easel/heimq", tag = "vX.Y.Z" }` and keep all heimq crates pinned to one tag.

2. TREAT THE heimq-handlers PUBLIC SURFACE AS A STABLE, SUPPORTED EMBEDDER CONTRACT. niflheim will consume: codec::{decode_request, decode_request_bytes, encode_response, encode_response_body}; produce::handle_async_with_context_and_config_store; the metadata / api_versions / init_producer_id handlers; and RecordBatchView::decode_all. niflheim accepts adopting the vendored `heimq_protocol` fork as its single kafka-protocol implementation (the niflheim goal is ONE shared vendored fork, not literally zero kafka-protocol). Please do not break these signatures without a version bump.

3. CONFIRM SASL OWNERSHIP. niflheim-7271ad92 originally assumed SASL would be served via the heimq engine, but heimq-handlers exposes NO SASL codec/dispatch (consistent with the heimq vision that SASL/TLS remain embedder-owned, capability-gated off in the heimq bin). Please confirm in writing that SASL is intentionally embedder-owned, so niflheim keeps SASL in-tree and rescopes 7271ad92 to ApiVersions/Metadata/InitProducerId only — OR, if heimq intends to expose an embedder-consumable SASL surface, say so and track it here.

Cross-repo note: heimq-236c0fc2 (the extraction) is already closed; this bead covers the remaining packaging + contract-stability + SASL-decision work that is the true gate on niflheim adoption.
notes: Final publication gate requested by Niflheim. Keep open until the release tag is remotely visible; implementable contract and SASL documentation is tracked as a child bead.
title: Publish heimq-handlers in a released tag with a stable embedder contract; confirm SASL ownership (niflheim adoption gate)

   - `pub fn handle(api_version, body, offset_store: &Arc<dyn OffsetStore>) -> Result<OffsetCommitResponse>`.
   - Imports `crate::storage::OffsetStore`.

   - `pub fn handle(_api_version, body, offset_store: &Arc<dyn OffsetStore>) -> Result<OffsetFetchResponse>`.
   - Imports `crate::storage::OffsetStore`.

   - Constructs `ConsumerGroupManager::new(config.clone())`, which internally builds an
     `Arc<dyn OffsetStore>` via `Arc::new(MemoryOffsetStore::new())` and is also
     overridable via `ConsumerGroupManager::with_offset_store(...)` for tests / future
     durable backends.

The previous review's BLOCKING findings ("offset_store.rs was not created", "handlers still use the concrete type", "server.rs not updated") describe a state that no longer exists — they refer to a state from before iteration commit 9362ca3 was made. description:
Fetch requests currently take an avoidable double-decode path. Router::route_async decodes the request header and then handle_fetch_long_poll decodes the FetchRequest to inspect max_wait_ms and requested offsets. When data is available it calls route(data), which decodes the header again and fetch::handle decodes the FetchRequest again. This is cheap per request but visible when consume issues many fetches. Refactor the fetch route so the decoded header/body and, where possible, decoded FetchRequest metadata are reused instead of decoding the same frame multiple times. Preserve max_wait_ms long-poll behavior and existing wire compatibility.

description:
Make heimq storage pluggable at runtime via URL scheme, without recompiling to swap. Three independent traits — LogBackend, OffsetStore, GroupCoordinatorBackend — each configured separately (default all memory://). Every backend exposes a BackendCapabilities descriptor so the broker can derive ApiVersions honestly and reject unsupported ops early.

Scope notes (from design review with codex):
- Log trait is structured (RecordBatchView + raw-bytes fast-path), NOT byte-opaque, because retention/compaction/timestamps/headers need parsed metadata.
- GroupCoordinatorBackend is richer than a simple load/save KV: it models live coordinator state (members, generations, assignments, sticky session semantics).
- Capability intersection is per-API/per-feature, not one global broker-wide meet. Startup validation for advertised ApiVersions; call-time rejection for per-request limits.
- Proxy-to-real-Kafka mode is explicitly out of scope — it needs a different trait family (UpstreamSession / CoordinatorBackend) and is a different architectural problem.

First durable backend lands as Postgres OffsetStore (smallest, highest value). Additional backends follow under separate beads.

To let niflheim delete its in-tree broker codec/handlers and reach zero direct kafka_protocol, extract these request-level handlers into heimq-broker (or a shared crate), generic over LogBackend + RequestContext, with a kafka_protocol-free public surface (or kafka_protocol contained inside heimq-broker). Also extend RecordBatchView to cover legacy v0/v1 message sets + multi-batch (decode_all) so niflheim's record-codec can fully migrate without regressing legacy/multi-batch produce.

description: Existing parity workloads exercise only the happy path. Add adversarial WorkloadDrivers in crates/heimq/tests/parity/workloads/ that drive heimq's idempotency/EOS enforcement and diff observable responses vs BOTH Apache Kafka and Redpanda: (a) duplicate_sequence: re-send a batch with an already-accepted base sequence -> DUPLICATE_SEQUENCE_NUMBER(46)/dedup; (b) out_of_order_sequence: gapped sequence -> OUT_OF_ORDER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER(45); (c) epoch_fence: two producers sharing transactional.id, second InitProducerId fences the first -> first commit returns INVALID_PRODUCER_EPOCH(47); (d) mid_txn_abort: produce in txn, abort, read_committed consume -> aborted records invisible. rdkafka manages sequences/epochs internally, so where needed encode raw RecordBatch/InitProducerId/Produce via kafka-protocol and a raw TCP client helper. In-scope: crates/heimq/tests/parity/workloads/{duplicate_sequence,out_of_order_sequence,epoch_fence,mid_txn_abort}.rs, workloads/mod.rs, driver.rs helpers. acceptance: 1. cd crates/heimq/tests/compat/franz_go && go build ./... succeeds. 2. cargo test -p heimq --test compat test_franz_go_produce_consume_consumer_group -- --nocapture passes with output lines including 'ok  produce-with-headers', 'ok  consume-headers-roundtrip', 'ok  delete-topic', 'ok  list-offsets'. 3. All 3 compat oracle tests pass: cargo test -p heimq --test compat -- --test-threads=1 passes.
description:
The franz-go compatibility oracle (crates/heimq/tests/compat/franz_go/main.go) currently tests only three scenarios: create-topic, produce, and consume-via-group. Three additional Kafka API behaviors are implemented in heimq but not covered by any Go oracle: record headers propagation, DeleteTopics (API key 20), and ListOffsets (API key 2, both HWM and LSO probes).

Add three new check() invocations to the franz-go oracle:

1. produce-with-headers: produce 3 records each with a 'x-trace' header (string UUID-like value) and an 'x-seq' header (string index). Use kgo.Record.Headers []kgo.RecordHeader.

2. consume-headers-roundtrip: consume those 3 records via a fresh consumer group and verify the x-trace and x-seq headers match what was produced (read via r.Headers slice).

3. delete-topic: use kadm.Client.DeleteTopics to delete the main test topic; verify the delete response contains no error; produce to the deleted topic should succeed (auto-create) but reading via consume-via-group should see only the newly produced records, not the pre-delete ones. Alternatively, verify that an explicit UNKNOWN_TOPIC_OR_PARTITION error is returned when fetch-after-delete is attempted — but auto-create makes this tricky. Simplest test: delete-topic returns no error.

4. list-offsets: after producing 5 records to the original topic, use kadm.Client.ListEndOffsets to verify the high-watermark is 5. Use kadm.Client.ListStartOffsets to verify LSO is 0.

In-scope files:
- crates/heimq/tests/compat/franz_go/main.go

Out-of-scope:
- crates/heimq/tests/compat.rs (no Rust changes needed)
- Any other oracle (sarama, java) — each oracle is self-contained
title: Expand franz-go oracle: record headers, DeleteTopics, ListOffsets

description:
The log trait needs to accept parsed batch metadata so backends can implement retention by timestamp, compaction by key, header-aware filtering, etc. Today produce handler (src/handler/produce.rs) and fetch handler (src/handler/fetch.rs) pass raw record-batch bytes through to Storage::append.

No decoding work beyond what produce handler already does via kafka_protocol::records — we wrap that result.

In-scope files:
- src/storage/record_batch_view.rs (new)
- src/storage/mod.rs (re-export)

- Calling it from handler/produce.rs (separate bead)
- Changing storage API (separate bead)

Niflheim cannot honestly route through a heimq-broker produce path until heimq-broker owns a version-neutral append core that takes topic, partition, raw record-batch bytes, backend capabilities, sequence/idempotence policy hooks, and returns base offset/error information.

description: Multi-consumer groups intermittently (~15-20%) entered a perpetual rebalance: a consumer looped JoinGroup(ok) -> SyncGroup(REBALANCE_IN_PROGRESS=27) forever, wedged at 0% CPU. This hung the KafkaJS compat oracle (tests/compat/kafkajs_oracle, multi-member-group test) and, because tests/compat.rs runs the oracle with no timeout, hung the whole ADR-002 workspace-test gate for 15+ minutes. Root-caused by debug-logging the live group state. Three compounding bugs in crates/heimq/src/consumer_group/{group.rs,mod.rs}: (1) the generation was bumped on EVERY join, including into an already in-progress rebalance, so each new joiner invalidated others' in-flight syncs (fix: bump only on Stable/Empty -> PreparingRebalance, via begin_rebalance()); (2) heartbeat() returned ILLEGAL_GENERATION (22) during a rebalance, and KafkaJS resets its member_id on 22, orphaning the old member into a ghost that never syncs (fix: return REBALANCE_IN_PROGRESS (27) for a known member while PreparingRebalance; unknown members still get 25 first); (3) the leader was elected only on the first-ever join and never updated, so a leader that completed an earlier generation then left stayed a dead leader that never SyncGroups, stalling the new rebalance (fix: leader = first member to join each generation, tracked via leader_generation; remove/expiry re-elect a live member). Fixed in commit e0ece69.
title: fix(consumer-group): perpetual rebalance loop hangs KafkaJS / multi-consumer groups

acceptance: 1. cargo test -p heimq --test integration test_rdkafka_transactional_produce_commit -- --nocapture passes. 2. cargo test -p heimq --test integration test_rdkafka_transactional_produce_abort -- --nocapture passes. 3. cargo test -p heimq --test integration -- --test-threads=4 shows no regressions.
description:
heimq has full transactional producer support (InitProducerId, AddPartitionsToTxn, EndTxn, WriteTxnMarkers, Fetch with isolation_level=1/LSO) but no integration tests exercise this path.

Add two async #[tokio::test] functions to crates/heimq/tests/integration.rs using rdkafka's ThreadedProducer (not FutureProducer — FutureProducer doesn't expose transactional methods; use rdkafka::producer::{ThreadedProducer, DefaultProducerContext} with the Producer trait for init_transactions/begin_transaction/commit_transaction/abort_transaction).

Test 1 — test_rdkafka_transactional_produce_commit:
1. Create ThreadedProducer with transactional.id=test-txn-commit, enable.idempotence=true, acks=all.
2. init_transactions(Duration::from_secs(5)).
3. begin_transaction().
4. Produce 3 records (key-0..key-2, val-0..val-2) to topic txn-commit-topic.
5. commit_transaction(Duration::from_secs(5)).
6. Consume via BaseConsumer (group=txn-commit-group) with isolation.level=read_committed.
7. Assert all 3 records are received with correct key/value pairs.

Test 2 — test_rdkafka_transactional_produce_abort:
1. Create ThreadedProducer with transactional.id=test-txn-abort, enable.idempotence=true, acks=all.
2. init_transactions(Duration::from_secs(5)).
3. begin_transaction().
4. Produce 3 records (aborted-key-0..2) to topic txn-abort-topic.
5. abort_transaction(Duration::from_secs(5)).
6. Consume via BaseConsumer (group=txn-abort-group) with isolation.level=read_committed, auto.offset.reset=earliest.
7. Poll for 500ms and assert zero records are received (aborted records must be invisible).

Note: ThreadedProducer requires explicit poll loop for delivery callbacks. Use the send/poll pattern or add a delivery timeout. The transactional send method is BaseRecord (not FutureRecord).

In-scope files:
- crates/heimq/tests/integration.rs (append the two new tests at the end)

Out-of-scope:
- Transaction coordinator logic, handlers — they exist in crates/heimq/src/handler/{init_producer_id,add_partitions_to_txn,end_txn}.rs and crates/heimq/src/transaction_state.rs — do not modify them
- No new crates, no Cargo.toml changes
title: Add rdkafka transactional producer integration tests (commit and abort)

Bead: heimq-cf148f58 — chore(verify): close epic heimq-700e47b2 once Phase 5 checkboxes flipped

Status: BLOCKED — cannot close epic this pass.

What is done
------------
- Verified all 22 child beads under epic heimq-700e47b2 have status=closed (grep against .ddx/beads.jsonl).
- Verified Phase 5 checkboxes in heimq/docs/helix/03-test/test-plan/test-plan.md are all flipped to [x] with bead-id traceability (commit f062a49a, closing bead heimq-0bde4555).

What is blocking
----------------
The bead description claims "integration suite passes; Phase 5 checkbox flip is the only remaining AC."
This is incorrect at HEAD (3306499). Running:

    cargo test --test integration -- --ignored

produces 40 passed; 1 failed:

    test_rdkafka_oversized_message ... FAILED
    panicked at tests/integration.rs:3343:9
    expected oversized payload (4194304 bytes) to round-trip because heimq
    enforces no message.max.bytes, got delivery error:
    KafkaError (Message production error:
    MessageSizeTooLarge (Broker: Message size too large)) after 13.290694ms

The epic's AC explicitly requires "cargo test --test integration -- --ignored
passes cleanly." That is not currently true, so closing the epic now would
record a false acceptance signal.

Notes for follow-up
-------------------
- The failing test was closed under bead heimq-cd905f86 (closing commit
  eb5c52c1) on 2026-04-25 with the assertion that heimq does not enforce
  message.max.bytes and the 4 MiB payload should round-trip. The current
  failure is a regression: the broker is now returning MessageSizeTooLarge
  for the 4 MiB payload at delivery time (~13 ms, not a hang).
- Root cause is most likely a recent change in heimq/src/handler/produce.rs
  or the produce request size validation path between eb5c52c1 and 3306499.
  Investigate and either:
    a) restore the no-server-side-cap contract the test pins, or
    b) update test_rdkafka_oversized_message and bead heimq-cd905f86 to
       reflect a new server-enforced cap (boundary assertion).
- After that test is green, this bead can re-claim and close epic
  heimq-700e47b2 with evidence linking f062a49a (checkbox flip) and the
  passing --ignored run.

Recommendation
--------------
Re-queue this bead after a separate fix bead lands for
test_rdkafka_oversized_message (regression triage on the produce path).

- Add env-controlled diagnostic output that reports at least: produced records/s, consumed records/s, consumer poll count, records per non-empty poll, approximate fetched payload MB/s, and configured consumer fetch settings.
- Add a mode or env knobs for larger consumer fetch batches (for example fetch.min.bytes / fetch.wait.max.ms / max.partition.fetch.bytes) without changing correctness assertions.

- Changing the default pass/fail policy.

acceptance: 1. PARITY_TESTS=1 cargo test -p heimq --test parity -- --test-threads=1 exits 0 with [PASS] for duplicate_sequence and out_of_order_sequence vs kafka AND redpanda. 2. heimq duplicate retry returns success while de-duplicating the batch (matching Kafka and Redpanda); heimq out-of-order sequence returns OUT_OF_ORDER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER (45), matching Kafka and Redpanda when the raw batch uses a valid offset delta.
description: Split from heimq-2c7afefc. That bead delivered the epoch_fence differential workload; mid-txn-abort is already covered by transactional_produce_roundtrip. Duplicate-sequence (DUPLICATE_SEQUENCE_NUMBER 46) and out-of-order-sequence (OUT_OF_ORDER_SEQUENCE_NUMBER 45) injection cannot be driven via rdkafka (it manages producer sequences internally), so they require a raw Kafka-protocol producer: open a TCP connection to the broker bootstrap, ApiVersions handshake, InitProducerId, then send Produce requests with hand-crafted RecordBatches carrying specific producer_id/epoch/base_sequence, and diff the returned per-partition error codes vs Apache Kafka + Redpanda. These enforcement paths are already wire-tested in heimq (contract_duplicate_sequence_returns_error, contract_out_of_order_sequence_returns_error); this bead adds the differential-vs-real-brokers layer. In-scope: a raw-protocol client helper under tests/parity/ (TCP framing, correlation ids, ApiVersions, InitProducerId, Produce encode via kafka-protocol crate) plus duplicate_sequence and out_of_order_sequence workloads registered in workloads::all(). Out-of-scope: Jepsen/multi-node.
notes: Parity implementation corrected the original expected duplicate error: the reference brokers both return success for a duplicate idempotent retry and do not append another record. The contract now asserts the consumer-visible de-duplication behavior.
title: FEAT-003: raw-protocol parity workloads for duplicate-seq (46) and out-of-order-seq (45)

description: handler/alter_configs.rs and handler/incremental_alter_configs.rs return success with a no-op for every requested resource (accept-and-ignore). A client setting e.g. retention.ms or cleanup.policy gets success but the value is silently dropped, and DescribeConfigs does not reflect it. Implement honest behavior: a per-topic config store in the broker; AlterConfigs (full replace) and IncrementalAlterConfigs (SET/DELETE/APPEND/SUBTRACT) update it; DescribeConfigs returns stored values; truly-unknown keys return INVALID_CONFIG (40) rather than false success. Honor a documented allow-list of topic-level keys (at least retention.ms, cleanup.policy, max.message.bytes) so the existing describe-configs/alter-configs compat oracles stay green. In-scope: src/handler/alter_configs.rs, incremental_alter_configs.rs, describe_configs.rs, a small per-topic config store, src/handler/tests.rs. Out-of-scope: actual retention/compaction enforcement; broker/cluster-level configs.

description:
Memory fetch currently builds a fresh Vec for every fetch and appends each selected record batch with extend_from_slice in Segment::read. That can reallocate and copies all fetched bytes before the FetchResponse encoder writes the response frame. Improve the in-memory fetch aggregation path without changing externally visible Kafka Fetch behavior. Prefer a small, low-risk change first: compute/capacity-plan the selected bytes and allocate once, or return a structure that avoids an intermediate copy if the trait shape already supports it. Keep retention and mid-batch fetch correctness intact.

- crates/heimq/src/storage/segment.rs
- crates/heimq/src/storage/partition.rs
- crates/heimq/src/handler/fetch.rs, only if a return-shape adjustment is needed
- crates/heimq/src/storage/mod.rs, only if trait exports need adjustment

Out-of-scope:
- Durable backend changes.
- Full zero-copy protocol encoder rewrite if it requires broad trait churn.
- Fetch semantic changes for transactions or isolation level.
title: Reduce in-memory fetch aggregation copy overhead

acceptance: test_encode_response_apiversions_no_flexible_header in heimq/src/protocol/codec.rs encodes at api_version=3, passes api_key=18 (per the FEAT-006 encode_response signature), and asserts total length = 8 + body_len_at_v3 with no tagged-fields trailer byte. The test must fail if a hypothetical implementation incorrectly adds a 0x00 trailer byte at offset 8 for ApiVersions v3.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit b5e508f (heimq-62ff3367 close).

File: heimq/src/protocol/codec.rs:545-552 (test_encode_response_apiversions_no_flexible_header)
Category: correctness
Severity: high

Description:
The test claims to pin CODEC-001 §Contract Validation #5 — that ApiVersions (api_key=18) responses must NOT carry a flexible response-header tagged-fields trailer even when the request is flexible v3 (CODEC-001 §216-219). However, the test invokes encode_response(1, 2, &ApiVersionsResponse::default()) — i.e., api_version = 2 (legacy). At v2, ApiVersions is not flexible at all, so the trailer would never be added regardless of any FEAT-006 special case. The assertion that bytes[8] = bytes[9] = 0x00 just exercises the legacy code path (correlation_id followed directly by error_code = 0). It cannot detect a buggy FEAT-006 implementation that fails to special-case ApiVersions at v3 and incorrectly emits a tagged-fields trailer. The test is vacuous against the very risk it claims to guard.

Note: encode_response signature today is (correlation_id, api_version, response). CODEC-001 §141-148 specifies it must gain an api_key parameter. Once that signature change lands, this test should be updated to pass api_key=18 at api_version=3 and assert the body starts immediately after correlation_id with no tagged-fields trailer byte (i.e., bytes[8] = first byte of the v3 response body, e.g., the throttle_time_ms high byte = 0x00, AND the total length must equal exactly [length(4)] + [corr_id(4)] + body_len_at_v3 with no extra +1).

Suggested fix:
Update test_encode_response_apiversions_no_flexible_header to:
1. Encode ApiVersionsResponse at api_version = 3 (flexible request header).
2. After FEAT-006 lands the api_key parameter on encode_response, pass api_key = 18.

acceptance: broker.rs no longer uses a hardcoded KAFKA_PORT for the host side. Two simultaneous 'PARITY_TESTS=1 cargo test --test parity' invocations on the same host succeed. Running with another process listening on 9092 still succeeds.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (governing bead hx-31a01378).
File: heimq/tests/parity/broker.rs:44-62
Category: drift
Severity: medium

Description: HARNESS-001-parity.md §Container Configuration explicitly states 'Exposed port: 9092 (mapped to ephemeral host port by testcontainers)'. The implementation uses .with_mapped_port(KAFKA_PORT, KAFKA_PORT.tcp()) to pin host port 9092 → container 9092 because --advertise-kafka-addr localhost:9092 is hardcoded. The author acknowledges this in the comment ('Use fixed host port = container port so Redpanda's advertised address is correct').

Consequences:
- Two parity test runs cannot execute concurrently on the same host (CI matrix, local + CI conflict, watch-mode reruns).
- Any local Kafka/Redpanda/heimq listening on 9092 (developer workstation) breaks the harness with an opaque container start failure.
- Future GHA self-hosted runners that share a Docker daemon will collide.

Suggested fix: let testcontainers assign an ephemeral host port (.with_exposed_port(KAFKA_PORT.tcp()) only, no .with_mapped_port), then learn the host port via container.get_host_port_ipv4(KAFKA_PORT).await? after start. Configure Redpanda to advertise that port — either by computing the port first and passing --advertise-kafka-addr localhost:<port> via .with_cmd (requires reserving a port outside testcontainers, e.g., bind a TcpListener and drop) OR by exec'ing 'rpk redpanda config set' inside the container after start to reset the advertised listener. The first approach is simpler: pre-reserve a free port, pass it both to .with_mapped_port and to --advertise-kafka-addr.
title: drift: parity Redpanda binds fixed host port 9092 instead of ephemeral

File: heimq/scripts/bench/kafka-perf-test.sh:83-84,90-91,107-108  README: heimq/scripts/bench/README.md (Prerequisites)
Category: drift

Description: The harness uses 'docker run --rm --network host ...' to reach 127.0.0.1:$HEIMQ_PORT on the host. This works on Linux Docker, but Docker Desktop on macOS/Windows has limited host-networking support: containers cannot routinely reach localhost services on the host even with --network host. README's 'Prerequisites: Docker' does not warn about this. A developer on a Mac will hit a confusing readiness-loop timeout with no clear pointer to the cause.

Option (a) is the smallest change and matches the bead's intent (legacy-version path only).

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 8f2aeb8.
File: heimq/docs/helix/02-design/contracts/API-001-kafka-protocol.md:27
Category: drift
Severity: medium

Description:
Commit 8f2aeb8 split the Request header bullet into 'non-flexible' and 'flexible v2' variants to align Transport and Framing with Version Policy on FEAT-006 flexible support. The parallel update was not made on the response side: line 27 still reads only '**Response header**: correlation_id.' with no mention of Response Header v1 (the flexible response header, which adds a TAG_BUFFER / tagged_fields block).

Per the Kafka wire protocol:
- Response Header v0: correlation_id
- Response Header v1: correlation_id, TAG_BUFFER (used by flexible-version responses; cf. KIP-482)

Flexible-version request/response headers come in pairs in Kafka and are encoded by the same kafka-protocol crate codec referenced in ADR-003. After the request-side fix, a reader of API-001 sees an asymmetric contract: requests get both header variants documented, responses do not. This is the same internal-contradiction class as the just-closed finding hx-708f92fe (Transport and Framing vs Version Policy), but in the inverse direction.

Note: ApiVersions is a documented Kafka quirk — it uses Response Header v0 even for flexible requests. Worth a footnote when the response header is split.

Suggested fix:
Update the Transport and Framing section to add a 'Response header (flexible v1)' bullet (correlation_id + tagged_fields block) in symmetry with the new flexible v2 request header bullet, referencing FEAT-006 and ADR-003. Optionally add a parenthetical noting that ApiVersions responses retain the v0 response header for compatibility.
title: drift: API-001 Response header missing flexible v1 variant after request-side update

acceptance: HARNESS-001-parity.md §Normalization Layer table or §Workload Driver Shape explicitly addresses CreateTime record timestamps; the diff harness produces zero diffs on the produce_fetch_roundtrip workload across two runs without manual exemption entries.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 0d3fb44.
File: heimq/docs/helix/02-design/HARNESS-001-parity.md:130-149
Category: correctness
Severity: medium

Description:
The normalization table covers log_append_time ('Replace with 0 when CreateTime semantics not in use') but not the record's own timestamp under CreateTime semantics. rdkafka producers default to setting message.timestamp.type=CreateTime via the producer's clock — the value is host wall-clock time at the moment of produce(). Two runs of produce_fetch_roundtrip (one against heimq, one against Redpanda) will record different CreateTime timestamps even though the workload is identical, producing spurious diffs on every observation.

The Observation enum carries the timestamp implicitly inside RecordConsumed if the harness records it; even if it does not, the harness still must decide whether to compare the value, normalize it, or ignore it.

Suggested fix:
Add a row to the normalization table: 'record CreateTime timestamp — Replace with sequential per-step index when CreateTime is in use; otherwise preserve.' Or, alternatively, document that record timestamps are explicitly excluded from the diff and update the RecordConsumed observation shape accordingly.
title: correctness: HARNESS-001 normalization table omits record-level CreateTime timestamps

acceptance: HARNESS-001-parity.md §Container Configuration specifies a non-floating image reference; the harness implementation passes that exact reference to testcontainers.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 0d3fb44.
File: heimq/docs/helix/02-design/HARNESS-001-parity.md:226-228
Category: correctness
Severity: medium

Description:
Design says: 'Image: docker.redpanda.com/redpandadata/redpanda:latest (pinned to a digest in tests/parity/redpanda-image.lock to prevent flake from upstream changes).' This is internally inconsistent:
- A ':latest' tag is by definition floating; it cannot be 'pinned'.
- testcontainers-rs takes an image string (name + tag/digest); it does not consult an external lockfile. A separate redpanda-image.lock cannot influence what testcontainers actually pulls.
- The NFR 'Less than 1% flake rate on the gating workload' depends on a stable image, so this gap matters.

Suggested fix:
Either (a) pin the image directly using a digest reference (e.g., docker.redpanda.com/redpandadata/redpanda@sha256:<digest>), with the lock file driving the digest used by the BrokerKind::Redpanda constructor; or (b) pin a specific version tag (e.g., :v25.x.y) with a renovate/dependabot job to bump it. Drop ':latest' from the design.
title: correctness: HARNESS-001 image pin strategy contradicts itself (':latest' tag with separate digest lock)

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 911126b.
File: heimq/docs/helix/03-test/test-plan/test-plan.md
Lines: Phase 7-pre, first checkbox: 'Codec primitives: compact string, compact bytes, compact array, unsigned varint, signed (zigzag) varint, tagged-fields block — round-trip property tests.'
Category: drift
Severity: high

Description:
ADR-003 (accepted same epic, commit 3d79b92) explicitly decides that heimq will NOT implement codec primitives — they are delegated entirely to the kafka-protocol crate. CODEC-001 §'Codec Primitives' restates this: 'heimq does not implement compact-string, compact-array, unsigned-varint, signed-zigzag-varint, or tagged-fields codec primitives'.

The test-plan checkbox would either (a) test the upstream crate's primitive correctness (out of heimq's scope), or (b) require heimq to own primitive code that the ADR explicitly forbids. Either path wastes implementer effort and produces tautological or out-of-scope tests.

Suggested fix:
Replace the 'Codec primitives' bullet with checkboxes targeting heimq's actual codec surface per CODEC-001 §'Contract Validation':
- is_flexible(api_key, api_version) boundary table tests (off-by-one on each entry).
- decode_request: legacy header path remains green; new flexible header path parses client_id (legacy nullable string) and consumes tagged-fields trailer.
- encode_response: flexible header path emits empty tagged-fields trailer after correlation_id.
- encode_response: ApiVersions v3 special case — no tagged-fields trailer in response header.
- Router roundtrip at one flexible API version (correlation_id preserved; response decodes via kafka-protocol crate).

Add a one-line note: 'Codec primitive correctness (varints, compact strings, tagged fields) is delegated to the kafka-protocol crate per ADR-003.'

acceptance: Either (a) the bench script gains an inline comment or commit clarifying that --data-dir is forward-defensive only and heimq storage is currently in-memory, OR (b) the --data-dir line is reverted with rationale, OR (c) a separate issue is filed root-causing actual metric repeatability variance with concrete evidence (e.g., back-to-back run logs showing differing throughput numbers and the underlying cause).
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 83f281b (last-commit).
File: heimq/scripts/bench/kafka-perf-test.sh:65
Category: correctness
Severity: medium

Description:
Commit 83f281b adds `--data-dir "$LOG_DIR/data"` to the heimq invocation in
the bench harness with the stated goal of preventing state carry-over and
fixing metric repeatability. Under the current heimq codebase the change is a
no-op:

  - `config.data_dir` (heimq/src/config.rs:21, default "./data") is read in
    exactly one place: `info!("Data directory: {}", config.data_dir.display())`
    at heimq/src/main.rs:44. It is never passed to any storage backend.
  - The actual storage backends are selected by `--storage-log`,
    `--storage-offsets`, and `--storage-groups`, which all default to
    `memory://` (heimq/src/config.rs:65-74). The bench script does not override
    them, so heimq writes nothing to disk regardless of `--data-dir`.
  - Each invocation of kafka-perf-test.sh kills its heimq child (lines 70-72)
    and starts a new one. Because storage is in-memory, the child’s state
    dies with the process. There is no on-disk `./data/` to clean up and no
    persistent state to leak between invocations.
  - The heimq binary likewise has no logic to mkdir `config.data_dir`; passing
    a non-existent directory is silently fine because nothing tries to use it.

Implications:
  1. The commit message — "Fixes metric repeatability: prior runs no longer
     accumulate messages on the topic" — is inaccurate. Repeated invocations
     were already isolated by virtue of memory-only storage and per-invocation
     process restart.
  2. If empirical metric instability was observed, the root cause is somewhere
     else (warm-up, JVM startup variance in the docker-side kafka tools, host
     noise, or interaction between sequential producer/consumer invocations
     where the consumer’s heimq has none of the producer’s data because they
     are different processes). That root cause is unfixed.
  3. The original bead hx-62f7121e’s premise ("writes persistent state to
     ./data/") was incorrect; the parallel-script comparison to compatibility-test.sh
     ("uses --memory-only for the same reason") is also misleading because
     --memory-only is itself only used for a log line and storage is already
     memory:// by default.
  4. The change is forward-defensive: if a disk-backed storage backend later
     consumes data_dir, the bench will already be per-run isolated. So removing
     it is not strictly necessary.

Suggested fix:
Pick one:
  (a) Keep the line and amend documentation / a follow-up commit to clarify
      that --data-dir is forward-defensive only, and that current heimq
      storage is in-memory regardless. Optionally add a comment in the bench
      script next to line 65 noting the rationale.
  (b) Revert the line if the bench harness should not carry hygiene measures
      for behavior heimq does not exhibit.
  (c) Independently investigate whether metric repeatability is actually
      flaky, and if so root-cause it (e.g., add warmup runs, fix the
      `--consumer`-after-`--producer` cross-process workflow which currently
      cannot work with memory-only storage).
title: correctness: bench --data-dir flag has no effect; storage backends default to memory://

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of heimq-9bcd03e1 (commit 0b01249f).
File: heimq/scripts/bench/kafka-perf-test.sh:64
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The harness starts heimq without --memory-only and without an isolated --data-dir, so heimq writes persistent state to ./data/ relative to the script's cwd. On subsequent invocations, the bench-producer-legacy topic and prior records still exist, violating the bead's assumption of a 'freshly-launched heimq'. This breaks repeatability: a second producer run accumulates messages on top of the first, and a consumer-only run after multiple producer runs sees N x records, not the profile's RECORDS count. Sister script compatibility-test.sh uses --memory-only for the same reason.
Suggested fix: pass --data-dir "$LOG_DIR/data" (creating a fresh dir under the per-run temp dir already in use) so each run is isolated. --memory-only is an alternative but would change which code paths are exercised by a perf bench.
title: correctness: bench harness leaves persistent heimq state between runs

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (governing bead hx-3d29e44e).
File: heimq/Cargo.toml:22-23
Category: drift
Severity: medium

Description: Commit ec2a8c2 moved serde and serde_json from [dev-dependencies] to [dependencies] so that the lib's test_support module (gated by feature 'test-support') can derive Serialize/Deserialize on DiffRecord. Net effect: serde + serde_json are now unconditionally required by the production heimq crate even though the only in-tree user is the feature-gated test_support module. 'cargo tree' confirms heimq directly depends on serde and serde_json at the top level. This contradicts the intent of the existing 'test-support' feature flag and the established pattern in this Cargo.toml (e.g., the optional 'postgres' dep tied to feature 'backend-postgres' via dep:postgres). It also leaves the production binary linking against crates that are exclusively for the parity harness's diff JSONL format.

Suggested fix: make both deps optional and gate them to the test-support feature, mirroring the backend-postgres pattern:

[dependencies]
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"], optional = true }
serde_json = { version = "1", optional = true }

[features]
test-support = ["dep:serde", "dep:serde_json"]

Verify with: 'cargo build --release' (no test-support) shows no serde/serde_json in 'cargo tree --no-default-features'. 'cargo test --lib' and 'cargo build --tests' (parity harness, which activates test-support via [dev-dependencies.heimq]) still build and pass.
title: drift: serde/serde_json should be optional + feature-gated to test-support

acceptance: CODEC-001-flexible.md updated: (a) table row v2 lists client_id as nullable-string (i16 len + bytes), (b) decode_request flexible-path text reads client_id as legacy nullable string then tagged-fields, (c) explicit reference to FEAT-006 FR #2 and KIP-482 added. Diff shows compact-nullable-string removed from request header v2 description.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 911126b.
File: heimq/docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CODEC-001-flexible.md
Lines: "Header Versions" table row v2 (~line 62) and "Updated decode_request interface" item 4 (~lines 88-92).
Category: bug / correctness
Severity: critical

Description:
CODEC-001 specifies that for the flexible request header v2, client_id is read as a 'compact-nullable-string (unsigned varint for len+1, then len bytes; varint 0 = null)'. This contradicts both KIP-482 and FEAT-006 functional requirement #2, which state that request header v2 retains the legacy nullable-string format for client_id (i16-length-prefixed).

Verified against the upstream kafka-protocol crate v0.15.1 (the crate ADR-003 selected): src/messages/request_header.rs encodes client_id via types::String (legacy nullable string) at all supported versions, including v2:
    let client_id = if version >= 1 {
        types::String.decode(buf)?
    ...

If implemented per CODEC-001 as written, every flexible request from a modern client would mis-parse client_id and shift the tagged-fields block / body offset by an unpredictable amount, breaking all flexible APIs.

Suggested fix:
1. Update the 'Header Versions' table row for v2 to: 'api_key i16, api_version i16, correlation_id i32, client_id nullable-string (i16 len + bytes, same as legacy), tagged-fields block'.
2. Update §'Updated decode_request interface' item 4 to read: 'If flexible: reads client_id as i16-length-prefixed nullable string (same encoding as the legacy header), then reads and discards the tagged-fields block (varint count followed by tag/length/value tuples).'
3. Add an explicit cross-reference to FEAT-006 FR #2 ('client_id still as nullable string') and KIP-482 (rationale: header was already on-the-wire and could not be retroactively re-encoded).

title: correctness: CODEC-001 wrongly specifies compact client_id in flexible request header v2

acceptance: Produce handler no longer holds a worker thread for the duration of a deferred backend commit; a single broker with N worker threads sustains many more than N concurrent in-flight appends; fjord's flush_coalescing benchmark shows coalescing degree scaling with client concurrency rather than broker worker-thread count; per-(producer,partition) ordering and idempotent sequencing preserved; all conformance/parity tests stay green; simple in-memory/log backends keep working via a default sync bridge.
description:
PROBLEM: LogBackend::append / PartitionLog::append are synchronous, so a Produce request blocks its broker worker thread until the backend returns. For backends that DEFER the durable commit — e.g. fjord's CoordinatorLogBackend, whose server-side flush buffer (group commit) holds the append until a batched commit_object completes — the number of in-flight appends, and therefore the achievable group-commit / object-coalescing degree, is bounded by the broker's worker-thread count rather than by client concurrency.

EVIDENCE (fjord): with client batching disabled (linger.ms=0, batch.num.messages=1), 32 concurrent producers coalesced only ~15x with 16 worker threads — the degree tracked worker count, not the 32 connections. Sync append caps the win of deferred-commit backends.

DESIGN: add an async append entry point on the storage seam (e.g. `async fn append_async(..) -> Future<(offset,count)>` that the produce router awaits, or offload the sync append onto a bounded spawn_blocking pool). The backend signals completion (offset assigned) via a Future/oneshot instead of blocking the calling worker. Keep the existing sync `append` working for simple backends via a default impl that bridges to the async one (or vice-versa). fjord's Flusher already uses a background flush thread + a per-append channel — it would hand back the receiver's Future directly instead of blocking on recv(). Must preserve per-(producer,partition) ordering and idempotent sequencing and not regress the in-memory/object-log backends.

Discovered while building fjord's TD-005 server-side flush buffering (fjord commits 1c33261 / 05ee78c).

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (governing bead hx-31a01378).
File: heimq/tests/parity/main.rs:25-63, heimq/tests/parity/README.md:50-53, HARNESS-001-parity.md §Structured Diff Format
Category: drift
Severity: medium

Description: Both HARNESS-001 §Structured Diff Format ('The diff output path is target/parity/<timestamp>-<workload>.jsonl.') and tests/parity/README.md ('Diffs are written to target/parity/<timestamp>-<workload>.jsonl (gitignored).') describe a JSONL file artifact. The implementation only println!s diffs to stdout and never opens, creates, or writes a file. There is no target/parity/ directory creation, no timestamp generation, no JSONL file handle anywhere in the harness.

This is a documentation-vs-code drift. For the scaffolding bead the workload set is empty so the missing artifact is not user-visible, but the very next workload bead will produce diffs that the docs promise are persisted. A consumer (CI artifact upload, post-mortem review, exemption authoring) reading either the README or the design will look in target/parity/ and find nothing.

Suggested fix: implement the documented behavior. After collecting diffs for a workload, open target/parity/<rfc3339-timestamp>-<workload>.jsonl (creating the directory if missing), serialize each DiffRecord to one line, and flush. Add 'target/' to .gitignore (already gitignored at repo level via Rust convention but verify). Alternatively, if persistence is intentionally deferred to a later bead, mark the README/design sections explicitly 'Deferred to <bead-id>' so docs and code do not lie.

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 0d3fb44.
File: heimq/docs/helix/02-design/HARNESS-001-parity.md:96-120, vs heimq/docs/helix/01-frame/user-stories/US-005-differential-parity-harness.md:18-21
Category: drift (design vs governing user story)
Severity: medium

Description:
US-005 acceptance criteria explicitly require 'The harness covers produce/fetch, consumer groups, idempotent producers, and transactions.' (US-005:21). The HARNESS-001 design implements only produce_fetch_roundtrip and consumer_group_lifecycle as initial workloads; idempotent_producer and transactions are listed under 'Expansion path (not in scope until FEAT-002 lands)' with a future status in exemptions.toml.

FEAT-003 frontmatter does declare 'depends_on: FEAT-002', so the dependency is pre-acknowledged at the feature level. But US-005 itself does not annotate the deferral, and a fresh reader treating US-005 as the gating spec for HARNESS-001 will conclude the design is incomplete. Authority order (Vision > PRD > Specs > Architecture > Designs) requires the design to either match the user story or to update the user story.

Suggested fix:
Either (a) update US-005 acceptance criteria to mark idempotent/transactional coverage as 'gated on FEAT-002 acceptance' (preferred — it stays accurate), or (b) split the deferred workloads into a sibling design (HARNESS-002) so HARNESS-001 itself can be marked 'satisfies US-005 gating workload only'.

acceptance: shellcheck clean; running the harness with a forced producer failure (e.g. unreachable broker) consistently exits non-zero and prints the summary marker chosen by the fix.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of heimq-9bcd03e1 (commit 0b01249f).
File: heimq/scripts/bench/kafka-perf-test.sh:35-36,130,162,175-179
Category: bug
Severity: medium
Description: The script uses set -euo pipefail (line 18) AND a FAILED accumulator pattern (FAILED=0 / fail() sets FAILED=1). But run_kafka_tool ends each error branch with 'return 1', and the function is invoked at the top level (lines 130 and 162) without ||-suppression. Under set -e, a function returning non-zero terminates the script immediately. Consequences: (a) if the producer fails, the consumer never runs even though the FAILED pattern implies both should be attempted; (b) the bottom summary block (lines 175-179) printing 'ALL CHECKS PASSED' vs 'ONE OR MORE CHECKS FAILED' is unreachable on the failure path -- the script just exits silently after the last 'FAIL:' line, with the summary marker missing.
Suggested fix: pick one model. Either (a) fail-fast: drop the FAILED variable and the bottom summary block; or (b) accumulate-and-report: invoke run_kafka_tool with '|| true' and rely on FAILED for the final exit status. Option (b) matches the apparent intent.
title: bug: set -e defeats FAILED accumulator and bottom summary block in bench harness

acceptance: diff.rs (or a sibling harness=true test target) contains a #[test] that round-trips DiffRecord through serde_json and asserts field equality. 'cargo test -p heimq' (no PARITY_TESTS) fails if any DiffRecord field is renamed or removed. main.rs no longer calls validate_schema().
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (governing bead hx-31a01378).
File: heimq/tests/parity/diff.rs:36-50, called from heimq/tests/parity/main.rs:16
Category: drift
Severity: medium

Description: HARNESS-001-parity.md §Quality Gates mandates 'The JSONL diff output schema is validated by a unit test in diff.rs.' The current implementation exposes 'pub fn validate_schema()' and invokes it from main.rs only inside the PARITY_TESTS=1 gate. As a consequence:
- A schema regression on DiffRecord serde derives will not be caught by 'cargo test' (default workflow), only by a Docker-equipped parity run.
- The check sits in production-shaped function form rather than the #[test] form mandated by the design.
- The failure mode contradicts the harness's own fail-fast posture (exemptions.rs is fail-fast, schema is not).

Suggested fix: convert validate_schema into '#[cfg(test)] mod tests { #[test] fn diff_record_round_trips_through_json() { ... } }' inside diff.rs and remove the runtime call in main.rs. Because the parity test has 'harness = false', the #[test] cannot live in the parity binary itself — move the DiffRecord struct (or a copy) into a small library module reachable from a normal #[test], or add a separate harness=true companion test target that exercises only the schema.
title: drift: parity diff schema validation should be #[test], not runtime check
